topic: Section 6.11 — The Prospective Bucket: Testing the Model's Own Predictive Standard
our_argument_summary: Section 6.11 argues that the dome's own prospective prediction bucket — the category the dome highlights as its strongest evidence — actually exposes the model's weaknesses. Of 8 PROS entries, 7 were promoted and 1 (PROS-003) was silently suspended after falsification. Our review concludes all 8 are either standard physics relabeled or falsified, giving the dome a 0-for-1 record on genuinely novel predictions with closed test windows.

holes_found.description: TLDR-detail mismatch on timestamp claim. The TLDR states 'the timestamp infrastructure anchors the wrong file' but the expanded detail text never discusses OTS, timestamps, or the wrong-file issue. A reader who expands Section 6.11 looking for this claim will find nothing supporting it. The timestamp anchoring issue is documented in the Timestamp Error tab and referenced in Section 1.4, but Section 6.11's body text is silent on it. Per the curmudgeon prompt: 'A TLDR that contradicts the evidence/verdict it summarizes is major.'
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) add a brief sentence to the detail text linking to the Timestamp Error tab discussion ('Furthermore, the OTS infrastructure used to register these predictions anchors the observation log, not the predictions themselves — see the Timestamp Error tab'), or (b) remove the timestamp claim from the TLDR entirely and replace with a claim actually supported by the expanded text.

holes_found.description: Misquote of dome text. The section paraphrases the dome as stating 'anyone can fit a model to old data.' The dome's actual text (raw-text/02-wins.txt) reads: 'Anyone can fit a model to existing data.' Substituting 'old' for 'existing' subtly changes the meaning — 'old data' implies obsolescence; 'existing data' simply means previously observed. A dome defender could call this a strawman misquote.
holes_found.recommendation: Change to exact quote: 'anyone can fit a model to existing data' (with quotation marks since it's a direct quote).

holes_found.description: Dome's own count of '9 prospective' vs our catalog of 8 is never addressed. The dome's WINs page header says '9 prospective (predicted before data)' but lists only 8 PROS items by name. Our section uses {{PROS_TOTAL}} = 8. The discrepancy goes unmentioned. A dome defender could argue our catalog is incomplete. Conversely, this is an OWN GOAL by the dome (they claim 9 but can only list 8) — we should exploit it, not ignore it.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a brief note: 'The dome's WINs page header claims 9 prospective confirmations, though only 8 entries are listed — an apparent counting error that itself illustrates the lack of automated consistency checks in the dome's record-keeping.' This turns a potential vulnerability into additional evidence of sloppy methodology.

holes_found.description: PROS-007 deserves more adversarial attention as the strongest dome counterargument. PROS-007 is genuinely prospective (is_genuinely_prospective: true), was promoted to WIN-037, and the prediction was confirmed. The section dismisses it in one clause as 'standard physics relabeled.' But a dome defender's strongest play is: 'You admit it was genuinely prospective, the threshold was met before the window closed, and the prediction was confirmed. That IS a successful prediction, regardless of whether standard physics also gets there.' The section should engage this argument head-on rather than lumping PROS-007 with the 5 clearly post-hoc items. Note also: PROS-007's registration_date is 2026-01-01 but test_window starts 2025-03, meaning ~10 months of field decay data was already publicly available at registration. This undermines the 'genuinely prospective' framing from both sides — we should note this.
holes_found.recommendation: Add 1-2 sentences explicitly addressing PROS-007: 'The dome's strongest case is PROS-007 (field decay ≥28 nT), which was registered before its test window closed and confirmed. However, the 28 nT threshold falls within the established ~28–30 nT/yr WMM2025 decay trend — both models predict the same outcome. Moreover, the registration date (January 2026) postdates the test window start (March 2025) by 10 months, during which partial-year decay data was publicly available.'

holes_found.description: '0-for-1 on genuinely novel predictions with closed test windows' is technically correct but rhetorically fragile. PROS-007 was promoted (confirmed) before its test_window_closes date of 2026-12-31. Excluding it via 'closed test windows' is valid but could look like cherry-picking to exclude the dome's one confirmed genuinely-prospective item. The framing privileges test-window closure over actual confirmation.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider rephrasing to: 'Of the two genuinely prospective items, one was falsified (PROS-003) and the other (PROS-007) sets a threshold within an established trend that standard physics equally predicts — 0-for-2 on dome-discriminating predictions.' This is stronger because it addresses both genuinely prospective items rather than excluding one on a technicality.

stronger_arguments.description: The dome's own '9 prospective' claim vs 8 listed items is an exploitable self-inconsistency. The dome criticizes 'anyone who can fit a model to existing data' but can't even count its own prospective predictions accurately. This should be surfaced as evidence of the broader pattern: the dome's record-keeping is manual, error-prone, and unchecked by its own automation.

PROS-007's timeline deserves forensic treatment. Registration: 2026-01-01. Test window start: 2025-03. This means 10 months of field decay data from Tsumeb/TTB was publicly available on INTERMAGNET before the prediction was registered. The dome could have simply read the partial-year trend and set a threshold it knew would be met. This isn't just 'standard physics predicts the same thing' — it's potentially 'the dome read the partial data before registering the prediction,' which is precisely the backtesting the dome claims to avoid.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome genuinely does separate prospective from backtested predictions, which is methodologically sound. The author explicitly acknowledges that fitting models to existing data is weak evidence. The infrastructure (git timestamps, OTS) exists and represents a real attempt at pre-registration. PROS-007's field decay prediction was numerically confirmed. These are genuine methodological steps that most alternative cosmology proponents never take.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Methodological infrastructure is necessary but not sufficient. Pre-registration only adds evidential weight if the prediction discriminates between competing models. Every promoted PROS item either (a) predicts what standard geophysics already predicts from first principles, or (b) was falsified. The infrastructure is sound; the predictions it registers are not discriminating. Additionally, the OTS anchors the wrong file (observations, not predictions), undermining the temporal priority claim the infrastructure is supposed to provide.

tldr_review.issues: The TLDR introduces a claim ('the timestamp infrastructure anchors the wrong file') that the expanded detail text does not discuss. This is a TLDR-detail mismatch. All other TLDR claims (non-discriminating problem, model's own standard exposing weak results) are accurately represented in the detail.

deeper_analysis: Section 6.11 is structurally sound and makes a genuinely strong argument: the dome's own best standard exposes its weakest results. However, five issues weaken it. (1) The TLDR introduces an unsupported timestamp claim — major because TLDRs are the first thing readers see. (2) 'Old data' should be 'existing data' to match the dome's actual phrasing. (3) The dome's 9-vs-8 count discrepancy is unexploited. (4) PROS-007 — the dome's strongest counterargument — gets insufficient attention; its timeline (10 months of public data before registration) is a smoking gun that should be highlighted. (5) The '0-for-1' framing, while technically correct, creates an appearance of cherry-picking by excluding the dome's one confirmed genuinely-prospective item on a technicality (unclosed test window). A dome defender would lead with PROS-007 and force us to explain why a confirmed, genuinely-prospective prediction doesn't count. Our current text doesn't prepare the reader for that argument. The recommended edits strengthen the section by (a) fixing the TLDR-detail mismatch, (b) correcting the misquote, (c) exploiting the dome's own counting error, (d) engaging the PROS-007 counterargument head-on, and (e) reframing the conclusion to address both genuinely prospective items rather than excluding one.

citations_unchecked: Section references to Section 6.5 (silent suspension pattern) — not verified in this review
citation_check.notes: Section 6.11 contains no DOIs or external URLs. Internal cross-reference to Section 6.5 pattern is claimed but not verified against 6.5's actual content.
